title: A childhood game we played in the street
summary: Narrate how a street game from your childhood worked.
topic: A childhood game we played in the street
prompt: Write 150–250 words about a game you and your friends played in the street as children. Explain the rules simply and describe one specific afternoon you played it. Use 'used to' for the habit and past simple for the specific day.
hintStarters: We used to play a game called…
I still remember one afternoon when…
sampleText: When I was a child in a small town in Argentina, we used to play a street game called rayuela, which is like hopscotch. The rules were simple. We drew eight squares on the road with a piece of chalk. Each square had a number. The first player threw a small stone at square one. Then she had to hop on one foot through the squares and pick up the stone on her way back. If you stepped on a line, you lost your turn. The next player started. We played in teams of two, and the team that finished all eight squares first was the winner. I still remember one summer evening. The sun was going down, and the road was warm under our feet. My team mate was a boy called Luca. He was younger than me, but he was very fast. I threw the stone too hard, and it landed outside the squares. Luca did not get angry. He just laughed and said it was my turn to draw new lines. We played until dark, and my mother shouted my name from the window. My knee was a little red, and my chalk fingers were white. But I was happy. We did not need anything more than a road and a stone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'Used to' for childhood habits
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'We used to play a street game called rayuela.' — Dùng cho thói quen trong quá khứ không còn nữa.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'We used to play a street game called rayuela.' Use 'used to' for a past habit that no longer happens.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past continuous for setting
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The sun was going down' — Past continuous tạo bối cảnh trước hành động chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The sun was going down' uses past continuous to set the scene before the main events.

sampleTranslationVi: Khi tôi còn nhỏ ở một thị trấn nhỏ tại Argentina, chúng tôi hay chơi một trò đường phố gọi là rayuela, giống ô ăn quan kiểu nhảy lò cò. Luật chơi đơn giản. Chúng tôi vẽ tám ô trên đường bằng một viên phấn. Mỗi ô có một số. Người chơi đầu tiên ném một viên đá nhỏ vào ô số một. Sau đó cô ấy phải nhảy lò cò qua các ô và nhặt viên đá khi quay lại. Nếu bạn dẫm lên vạch, bạn mất lượt. Người chơi tiếp theo bắt đầu. Chúng tôi chơi theo đội hai người, và đội nào đi hết tám ô trước là thắng. Tôi vẫn nhớ một buổi tối mùa hè. Mặt trời đang lặn, và mặt đường ấm dưới chân chúng tôi. Đồng đội của tôi là một cậu bé tên Luca. Cậu ấy nhỏ tuổi hơn tôi nhưng rất nhanh. Tôi ném viên đá quá mạnh và nó rơi ngoài các ô. Luca không cáu. Cậu ấy chỉ cười và bảo tôi đến lượt vẽ lại các đường. Chúng tôi chơi cho đến khi trời tối, và mẹ tôi gọi to tên tôi từ cửa sổ. Đầu gối tôi hơi đỏ, ngón tay tôi trắng phấn. Nhưng tôi vui. Chúng tôi không cần gì hơn một con đường và một viên đá.

options: We used to play a street game called rayuela.
We used to played a street game called rayuela.
We use to play a street game called rayuela.
We were used to play a street game called rayuela.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' for past habits.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct past simple sentence.
options: We drawn eight squares on the road with a piece of chalk.
We drew eight squares on the road with a piece of chalk.
We draw eight squares on the road with a piece of chalk.
We drawed eight squares on the road with a piece of chalk.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Draw' is irregular: drew, drawn. Past simple = 'drew'.

options: If you step on a line, you lost your turn.
If you stepped on a line, you lose your turn.
If you stepped on a line, you lost your turn.
If you stepping on a line, you losing your turn.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past simple in both clauses describes the rules as remembered.
mcQuestions.hint: Keep both clauses in the same past tense.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the correct comparative?
options: He was more young than me, but he was very fast.
He was the youngest than me, but he was very fast.
He was young than me, but he was very fast.
He was younger than me, but he was very fast.
mcQuestions.explanation: Short adjectives form comparatives with -er + than.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the correct past continuous form.
options: The sun was going down, and the road was warm under our feet.
The sun is going down, and the road was warm under our feet.
The sun going down, and the road was warm under our feet.
The sun has gone down, and the road was warm under our feet.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past continuous for the slow background action; past simple for the state.

options: We played until darkly.
We played until dark.
We are playing until dark.
We had played until darkness.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Until + noun/adjective of time' is a fixed pattern; past simple matches the memory.

options: My team mate was a boy calling Luca.
My team mate was a boy he is called Luca.
My team mate was a boy called Luca.
My team mate was a boy what called Luca.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A boy called Luca' is a reduced relative clause — natural English.
mcQuestions.hint: 'A boy called X' = 'a boy whose name is X'.

options: I threw the stone too hard, because it landed outside the squares.
I threw the stone too hard, but it landed outside the squares.
I threw the stone too hard, while it landed outside the squares.
I threw the stone too hard, so it landed outside the squares.
We did not need anything more than a road and a stone.
We did not need more anything than a road and a stone.
We not needed anything more than a road and a stone.
We need not anything more than a road and a stone.
mcQuestions.explanation: Negative past simple with 'did not + base verb', and 'anything more than' is the natural order.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which closing line is concrete and personal?
options: In conclusion, childhood games are important.
But I was happy.
It is important to note that simple games are best.
Throughout history, children have played in streets.
mcQuestions.explanation: A short, personal line is stronger than generic phrasing.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid 'In conclusion' or 'It is important to note'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Do children today have fewer chances to play freely in the street than children twenty years ago? What changes if they do?
